steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Need to pull a single file out of a backup archive — /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz has our old nginx config somewhere inside, should be at etc/nginx/nginx.conf relative to the archive root. Just extract that one file to /home/user/restored/, don't need the rest of the tarball unpacked.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@6c297493ed57:/home/user#

Analysis: I'm at /home/user# prompt. I need to: 1) Verify the backup archive exists at /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz, 2) Check if the target directory /home/user/restored/ exists or create it, 3) Extract the specific file etc/nginx/nginx.conf from the archive to /home/user/restored/
Plan: First, I'll list the backups directory to confirm the archive exists. Then I'll check/create the restored directory. Finally, I'll use tar with the --extract flag to pull out just the etc/nginx/nginx.conf file from the archive into the target directory.

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:
WARNINGS: - Extra text detected before JSON object

New Terminal Output:

root@6c297493ed57:/home/user# ls -lh /home/user/backups/
total 4.0K
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1.8K Apr 29 09:10 services-2024.tar.gz
root@6c297493ed57:/home/user# mkdir -p /home/user/restored/
root@6c297493ed57:/home/user# tar -xzf /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz -C /home/user/restored/ etc/nginx/nginx.conf
root@6c297493ed57:/home/user# ls -lR /home/user/restored/
/home/user/restored/:
total 4
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Apr 29 09:11 etc

/home/user/restored/etc:
total 4
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Apr 29 09:11 nginx

/home/user/restored/etc/nginx:
total 4
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 173 Apr 29 09:10 nginx.conf
root@6c297493ed57:/home/user#

steps.message:
Analysis: The task has been completed successfully. The archive existed at /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz, the /home/user/restored/ directory was created, and the specific file etc/nginx/nginx.conf was extracted from the archive. The file is now located at /home/user/restored/etc/nginx/nginx.conf with the directory structure preserved. The file is 173 bytes in size and has proper permissions (rw-r--r--).
Plan: Verify the contents of the extracted nginx.conf file to confirm it's valid, then mark the task as complete.

Analysis: The task has been completed successfully. The nginx configuration file has been extracted from the backup archive /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz to /home/user/restored/. The file is located at /home/user/restored/etc/nginx/nginx.conf and contains valid nginx configuration content. The extraction preserved the directory structure (etc/nginx/) as it existed in the archive.
Plan: The task is complete. The single file etc/nginx/nginx.conf has been successfully extracted from the tar.gz archive to the /home/user/restored/ directory without unpacking the rest of the archive.
